content:
8 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Docker Compose gets called a “beginner tool” as soon as people mention Kubernetes. That’s not fair. In real DevOps jobs staging environments, internal tools, small and mid size company setups, matching your local dev to production Compose is still what runs on a lot of live servers, including ones I manage myself. This guide isn’t about basic syntax. It’s about the patterns, mistakes, and workflows that actually matter once you’re using Compose for real traffic and real deadlines.
Why Compose Still Matters in a Kubernetes World
Kubernetes solves problems you don’t have yet: multi node scheduling, self healing at scale, complex rollout strategies across hundreds of pods. Most SME infrastructure a WordPress site, an internal dashboard, a couple of APIs and a database doesn’t have those problems. It has a single VPS, a handful of services, and a team that needs to reason about the whole stack without a control plane.
Compose gives you:

• A single declarative file describing the entire application

• Predictable local to prod parity

• Fast iteration without cluster overhead

• A shallow enough learning curve that a two person ops team can actually own it
The honest rule of thumb: reach for Kubernetes when you need to schedule across multiple machines or need advanced autoscaling/self healing. Everything below that line, Compose is often the right choice, not just the easy one.
The Mental Model: Compose Is a Declarative Diff Engine
The biggest shift in thinking, once you move past “docker-compose up”: Compose doesn’t just start containers, it reconciles state. Every time you run docker compose up -d, Compose compares the desired state in your YAML against the current state of running containers and only touches what changed. This matters because:

• Changing an environment variable recreates only that service

• Changing a build context triggers a rebuild only for affected images

• Unrelated services are left running untouched
Understanding this stops a lot of unnecessary docker compose down && up cycles that needlessly kill healthy containers and cause avoidable downtime.
Multi Environment Configuration (Without Copy-Pasting YAML)
The pattern most teams get wrong early on: maintaining separate docker-compose.dev.yml, docker-compose.staging.yml, docker-compose.prod.yml files that drift out of sync. The correct approach is base + override.
# docker-compose.yml (base - shared across all environments)
services:
app:
build: .
image: myapp:latest
networks:
- app-net
depends_on:
- db db:
image: postgres:16
volumes:
- db-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data
networks:
- app-net
volumes:
db-data:
networks:
app-net:
# docker-compose.staging.yml (override)
services:
app:
environment:
- APP_ENV=staging
ports:
- "8080:8080" db:
environment:
- POSTGRES_DB=staging_db
Run it with:
docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.staging.yml up -dCompose merges these files field-by-field. Scalars get overwritten, lists get replaced (not merged a common surprise), and maps get merged key by key. This is the same base+override idea I used when writing about staging environments with Nginx the principle is identical whether you’re overriding an Nginx vhost or a Compose service definition: one source of truth, environment-specific deltas layered on top.
Environment Variables and Secrets: Getting the Layering Right
Compose resolves variables from multiple sources, and the precedence order trips people up constantly. From highest to lowest priority:

• Shell environment variables

• --env-file flag on the CLI

• environment: key in the Compose file

• env_file: key in the Compose file

• .env file in the project root (used for variable substitution inside the YAML itself, not injected into containers automatically)
That last point is the one that catches people out: a .env file in your project root is read by the Compose CLI itself to substitute ${VAR} placeholders in the YAML it is not automatically passed into the container's runtime environment unless you also reference it via environment: or env_file:.
services:
app:
image: myapp:${APP_VERSION} # substituted from .env at parse time
env_file:
- .env.runtime # actually injected into the containerFor secrets, avoid baking them into any committed file at all. Compose supports a secrets: top-level key backed by files, which is a meaningfully safer default than environment variables (env vars leak into docker inspect, process listings, and crash logs):
services:
app:
secrets:
- db_passwordsecrets:
db_password:
file: ./secrets/db_password.txt
./secrets/ goes in .gitignore, obviously and if you're managing this across a small team, a password manager or a vault (even something lightweight) beats passing .env files over Slack.
Health Checks and Startup Ordering
depends_on alone only controls start order, not readiness. A database container can report "started" in under a second while Postgres is still initializing and your app container will happily connect, fail, and crash-loop. This is one of the most common sources of flaky "works on my machine" bugs.
Fix it with health checks and conditional dependencies:
services:
db:
image: postgres:16
healthcheck:
test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
interval: 5s
timeout: 5s
retries: 5 app:
build: .
depends_on:
db:
condition: service_healthy
Now app won't start until Compose has confirmed db's health check passes, not just that the container process exists. For any service with a real startup routine migrations, cache warm-up, index building this single change eliminates a whole category of race-condition bugs that otherwise only show up under specific timing, making them miserable to reproduce.
Networking: Service Discovery Without Thinking About It
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Compose creates a default bridge network per project, and every service can reach every other service by its service name as a DNS hostname no manual linking, no IP management.
services:
app:
environment:
- DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:pass@db:5432/mydb # "db" resolves automatically
db:
image: postgres:16Where this gets more advanced is network segmentation. Not every service needs to talk to every other service, and not every service needs to be reachable from the host. A common production-adjacent pattern:
services:
nginx:
networks: [frontend]
ports: ["443:443"] app:
networks: [frontend, backend]
db:
networks: [backend] # never exposed to frontend or host
networks:
frontend:
backend:
db here has no route to the internet-facing network at all it's only reachable from app. This is the same reasoning behind putting a database on a private subnet in cloud networking; Compose just gives you a lighter weight version of that segmentation on a single host.
Volumes: Know the Difference Between Named Volumes and Bind Mounts
This distinction matters more in daily ops than most people treat it:

• Named volumes (db-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data) are managed by Docker, live under /var/lib/docker/volumes/, and are the right choice for stateful data you want Docker to own and that survives container recreation cleanly.

• Bind mounts (./src:/app/src) map a host path directly into the container. Great for local dev live-reload; risky for production data because the host filesystem's permissions, path existence, and backup strategy all become your problem, not Docker's.
A mistake I see often: using a bind mount for a production database “because it’s easier to back up.” It isn’t you now need to worry about file permission mismatches between host UID/GID and container UID/GID, and about the container not fully controlling write consistency during a snapshot. Named volumes plus a proper backup job (docker run --rm -v db-data:/data -v $(pwd):/backup alpine tar czf /backup/backup.tar.gz /data, or a scheduled pg_dump inside the container) is the more robust pattern the same layered thinking behind a proper 3-2-1 backup approach, just scoped to a single volume instead of a whole server.
Resource Limits: Don’t Let One Container Take Down the Host
On a shared VPS running multiple Compose projects, an unbounded container can starve everything else of memory or CPU. Compose (v2, using the deploy.resources key, honored even outside Swarm mode since Compose v2.x with docker compose up) lets you cap this:
services:
app:
deploy:
resources:
limits:
cpus: "1.0"
memory: 512M
reservations:
memory: 256MThis is cheap insurance. A single memory leak in one container without limits can trigger the OOM killer at the host level, which doesn’t discriminate it can just as easily kill your database as the leaking app.
The Debugging Workflow That Actually Saves Time
When something’s broken, this is the order that gets you to the answer fastest, not the order most people default to:
docker compose ps # is it even running, and is it healthy?
docker compose logs -f --tail=100 app # recent logs, streaming
docker compose exec app sh # get a shell inside the running container
docker compose exec db pg_isready # check a dependency directly, don't assume
docker compose top app # what's actually running inside, PIDs and all
docker compose config # render the FINAL merged config — catches override mistakes instantlyThat last one, docker compose config, is underused. When you're layering base + override files, it prints the fully resolved YAML Compose will actually use. If a variable didn't substitute the way you expected, or an override didn't merge the way you expected, this command shows you the truth before you waste time debugging the running container instead of the config.
Compose in CI/CD: Build Once, Deploy the Same Artifact
Press enter or click to view image in full size

A subtle but important discipline: don’t docker compose build separately in every environment. Build once in CI, push a tagged image, and have every environment's Compose file reference that exact tag never latest in anything beyond local dev.
# docker-compose.prod.yml
services:
app:
image: registry.example.com/myapp:${GIT_SHA}
# no `build:` key here — prod never builds, only pullsIn your CI pipeline:
docker build -t registry.example.com/myapp:$GIT_SHA .
docker push registry.example.com/myapp:$GIT_SHA
# on the server:
GIT_SHA=$GIT_SHA docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml pull
GIT_SHA=$GIT_SHA docker compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -dThis guarantees the exact bytes tested in CI are the exact bytes running in production no “it built differently on the server” surprises. It’s the same principle GitOps pipelines are built around, just without the extra machinery a full CI/CD platform adds; a good bridge concept if you’re about to go deeper into GitHub Actions-driven deployments next.
Common Pitfalls Checklist
A quick list worth keeping pinned somewhere:

• depends_on without condition: service_healthy : silently allows race conditions on startup.

• Bind-mounting production data directories : couples your data lifecycle to host filesystem quirks.

• No resource limits on a shared host : one runaway container can take down every other service.

• .env assumed to be injected automatically : it's only used for YAML substitution unless explicitly wired into environment:/env_file:.

• Using latest tags in production Compose files : makes rollbacks and reproducibility guesswork.

• Not running docker compose config before troubleshooting a multi-file override setup wastes time debugging the wrong layer.

• Forgetting docker system prune hygiene : dangling images and volumes from old builds quietly eat disk space on long-running VPS hosts.
Quick Reference Cheat Sheet
docker compose up -d # start in detached mode
docker compose down # stop and remove containers, keep volumes
docker compose down -v # also remove named volumes (careful!)
docker compose restart app # restart a single service
docker compose logs -f app # follow logs for one service
docker compose exec app
